svg.text: Advanced Chunking — beyond fixed sizes
Semantic, language-based, and context-aware chunking — when, why, and how
1. Why fixed-size still falls short
"That night she dreamed, as she did often, that
she was finally an Olympic champion"

g.text: That night she dreamed, as she did often, that
she was finally an Olympic champion

text.tspan: The split happens inside a single thought.

svg.text: "that" belongs with what follows, but ends up in
the previous chunk — the next chunk loses
grammatical and semantic continuity.
Overlap helps, but a smarter splitter wouldn't break here at all.
2. Semantic chunking — split where meaning shifts

g.text: Process the document sentence by sentence
If distance < threshold → topics are still
related → append sentence to current chunk
shifted → close chunk, start a new one
Greg Kamradt's "5 Levels of Text Splitting"
When the cosine spike crosses the red line, a chunk boundary is drawn.

svg.text: 3. Semantic chunking — trade-offs

g.text: ● Follows the author's train of thought
● Higher recall AND precision (one idea per vector)
In benchmarks: often 5–15% retrieval lift over fixed-size
● Embedding for every sentence at index time → slow
● Repeated cosine calculations across the doc
Pre-processing cost is one-time, but the bill adds up at scale

svg.text: 4. Language-based chunking — let an LLM cut the text
Document
LLM
● Prompt the LLM to split the document.
● Give it rules: keep concepts together,

text.tspan: break when new topic begins, etc.

svg.text: ● Performs very well; with small models
(Haiku, GPT-4o-mini), economically viable.
Highest quality, highest pre-processing cost.
5. Context-aware chunking — the "first improvement" to try
The idea
Before storing each chunk, run an LLM to attach extra context describing where this chunk sits in the doc.
Each chunk becomes more self-contained → retrieval finds it even when the query words don't match the chunk itself.
Worked example: a blog post about an app launch
"Reflections on Launching Our First App"
context: app launch title.
After six months of development, we launched...
context: launch announcement.
The biggest lessons were around user feedback...
context: summary of key development lessons.
Thanks to Daniel, Sarah, Mike...
context: list of supporters and contributors.
Now a query for "who helped build the app?" matches the fourth chunk via its context, even though the chunk just
contains names. Without context, that retrieval would have failed.

g.text: context chunk by chunk (one LLM call each).
no impact on query speed — it's all baked
context labels, not just chunk words.
just from doing this on top of existing chunkers.

svg.text: 6. Choosing an approach — a decision tree

g.text: ● Layers on top of ANY chunker

svg.text: 7. 2026 state of the art & toolbelt
What's actually shipping in production
● Anthropic Contextual Retrieval — the canonical implementation of context-aware chunking (prompt caching makes it cheap)
● LangChain SemanticChunker • LlamaIndex SemanticSplitterNodeParser — off-the-shelf semantic chunking
● Late chunking (Jina) — embed the whole doc with long-context model, then pool per chunk → preserves global context for free
● Agentic chunking (Kamradt's "Level 5") — an LLM iteratively groups propositions into "themed buckets"
